text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic appendectomy for acute appendicitis with the appendix in a retrocecal position. The cecum is mobilized to improve visibility of the appendix, while careful attention is paid to the nearby right ureter, visible with peristalsis. Using monopolar cautery, the surgeon dissects the peritoneum, aided by carbon dioxide insufflation, which reveals avascular planes. Traction and counter-traction techniques help expose the appendix gradually.

The surgeon uses cautery to incise the lateral attachment of the peritoneum in the retroperitoneum, revealing the appendix's location near the subhepatic region. The dissection progresses close to the liver, with facial attachments being divided to visualize the appendix. Traction and counter-traction help open the plane, allowing identification of the appendix's length and small attachments near its base. The terminal ileum is visible near the ileocecal junction, requiring careful incision to avoid damage.

The surgeon performs further dissection to rotate the cecum and appendix away from retroperitoneal structures, ensuring clear visualization. Small fascial attachments covering the appendix are incised using monopolar cautery, exposing the appendix and mesoappendix for appendectomy. The cecum is identified and used as a landmark to guide dissection, with gradual removal of tissues over the anti-mesenteric border of the appendix, revealing its bare structure.

The surgeon identifies and holds thin facial attachments between the appendix and surrounding structures, using short bursts of cautery to dissect and open a window around the mesoappendix and appendix. The camera is cleared to improve visibility, allowing for a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to continue separating the attachments. Monopolar energy is used to take down remaining attachments, revealing the tip of the appendix. The surgeon applies traction to better visualize the appendix tip, while being cautious of the cecum to avoid injury. The dissection proceeds with a focus on maintaining a safe margin from the cecum, using cautery to remove attachments.

The surgeon observes the translucency of the opposite side, indicating the absence of major structures, allowing for safe coagulation. With traction applied, small thin attachments are visible, and the mesoappendix begins to appear in the plane with vessels running behind. The surgeon identifies and coagulates small vessels using a bipolar device and a ligature for efficiency. The appendix is twisted to isolate it from the mesoappendix, ensuring it can be easily removed through a tenon port with an endo loop. The cecum is clearly identified to guide meticulous dissection at the appendix base, avoiding thermal damage to nearby structures like the terminal ileum. Additional adhesions are removed with an energy source, revealing small vessels branching from the appendicular branch of the ileocolic artery.

The surgeon carefully dissects the anterior and posterior leaves of the peritoneum near the base of the appendix, avoiding excessive use of monopolar cautery to prevent damage. Small attachments are meticulously taken down, creating a clear window for adhesiolysis. The appendix is freed, allowing for the application of an endo loop. The cecum is identified and manipulated to ensure safe dissection, with visible tinea on its surface. The surgeon performs a milking maneuver at the base of the appendix to push any fecolith distally before securing the endo loop.

The surgeon palpates the firm base of the appendix, avoiding the cecal wall to prevent a cecal fistula. Two endo loops are applied proximally, spaced 5 mm apart, followed by a third distal loop to prevent fecolith spillage. A butadiene-soaked gauze is used for chemical cauterization. The small bowel is examined for Meckel's diverticulum using a bubble walk technique. The appendix is removed through a ten mm port using a claw forceps, ensuring it does not contact the skin.
